revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also! ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
[[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''{{ill|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|ja|伴大納言絵詞}}'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding {{ill|Chion-in|ja|知恩院}}]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} and were first established in the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁}} 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)}} were abolished under {{ill|Emperor Kanmu|ja|桓武天皇}}, the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific {{ill|Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|ja|官位相当}}. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In {{ill|Kanpyō|ja|寛平}} 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by {{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦}} 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the {{ill|Gyōbushō|ja|刑部省}} (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例}}'', which diverged from traditional {{ill|Ritsuryō law|ja|律令}}. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''{{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当}}'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士}}, a military organization of the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} era. Under the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}, the establishment of the {{ill|Rokuhara Tandai|ja|六波羅探題}} further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, their authority was transferred to the {{ill|Samurai-dokoro|ja|侍所}} of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|ja|貞和}} era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; {{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Daiyū|ja|大尉}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}. ; {{ill|Shōyū|ja|少尉}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}} or {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashōmon (short story)|ja|羅生門}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|ja|藪の中}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heian Kyō Alien|ja|平安京エイリアン}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣}} * {{ill|押領使|ja|押領使}}

revision.text:
[[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''{{ill|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|ja|伴大納言絵詞}}'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding {{ill|Chion-in|ja|知恩院}}]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制}} officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} and were first established in the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁}} 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)}} were abolished under {{ill|Emperor Kanmu|ja|桓武天皇}}, the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific {{ill|Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|ja|官位相当}}. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In {{ill|Kanpyō|ja|寛平}} 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by {{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦}} 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the {{ill|Gyōbushō|ja|刑部省}} (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例}}'', which diverged from traditional {{ill|Ritsuryō law|ja|律令}}. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''{{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当}}'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士}}, a military organization of the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政}} era. Under the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}, the establishment of the {{ill|Rokuhara Tandai|ja|六波羅探題}} further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, their authority was transferred to the {{ill|Samurai-dokoro|ja|侍所}} of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|ja|貞和}} era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; {{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Daiyū|ja|大尉}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}. ; {{ill|Shōyū|ja|少尉}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}} or {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashōmon (short story)|ja|羅生門}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|ja|藪の中}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heian Kyō Alien|ja|平安京エイリアン}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣}} * {{ill|押領使|ja|押領使}}

[[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''{{ill|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|ja|伴大納言絵詞}}'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding {{ill|Chion-in|ja|知恩院}}]]

Under the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}, the establishment of the {{ill|Rokuhara Tandai|ja|六波羅探題}} further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, their authority was transferred to the {{ill|Samurai-dokoro|ja|侍所}} of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|ja|貞和}} era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; {{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Daiyū|ja|大尉}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏}}. ; {{ill|Shōyū|ja|少尉}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}} or {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashōmon (short story)|ja|羅生門}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|ja|藪の中}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heian Kyō Alien|ja|平安京エイリアン}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣}} * {{ill|押領使|ja|押領使}}

revision.text:
[[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''{{ill|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|ja|伴大納言絵詞|12=draft}}'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding {{ill|Chion-in|ja|知恩院|12=draft}}]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制|12=draft}} officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|12=draft}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} and were first established in the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁|12=draft}} 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|12=draft}} were abolished under {{ill|Emperor Kanmu|ja|桓武天皇|12=draft}}, the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=draft}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|12=draft}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific {{ill|Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|ja|官位相当|12=draft}}. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|12=draft}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=draft}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In {{ill|Kanpyō|ja|寛平|12=draft}} 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by {{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦|12=draft}} 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the {{ill|Gyōbushō|ja|刑部省|12=draft}} (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|12=draft}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=draft}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|12=draft}}'', which diverged from traditional {{ill|Ritsuryō law|ja|律令|12=draft}}. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''{{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当|12=draft}}'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|12=draft}}, a military organization of the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政|12=draft}} era. Under the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府|12=draft}}, the establishment of the {{ill|Rokuhara Tandai|ja|六波羅探題|12=draft}} further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, their authority was transferred to the {{ill|Samurai-dokoro|ja|侍所|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|12=draft}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|ja|貞和|12=draft}} era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=draft}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=draft}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; {{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当|12=draft}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=draft}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=draft}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=draft}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Daiyū|ja|大尉|12=draft}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=draft}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=draft}}. ; {{ill|Shōyū|ja|少尉|12=draft}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|12=draft}} or {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|12=draft}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=draft}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=draft}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=draft}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashōmon (short story)|ja|羅生門|12=draft}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|ja|藪の中|12=draft}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heian Kyō Alien|ja|平安京エイリアン|12=draft}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|12=draft}} * {{ill|押領使|ja|押領使|12=draft}}

''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=draft}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Daiyū|ja|大尉|12=draft}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=draft}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=draft}}. ; {{ill|Shōyū|ja|少尉|12=draft}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|12=draft}} or {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|12=draft}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=draft}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=draft}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=draft}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashōmon (short story)|ja|羅生門|12=draft}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|ja|藪の中|12=draft}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heian Kyō Alien|ja|平安京エイリアン|12=draft}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|12=draft}} * {{ill|押領使|ja|押領使|12=draft}}

revision.text:
[[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''{{ill|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|ja|伴大納言絵詞|12=draft}}'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding {{ill|Chion-in|ja|知恩院|12=draft}}]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制|12=draft}} officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|12=draft}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} and were first established in the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁|12=draft}} 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|12=draft}} were abolished under {{ill|Emperor Kanmu|ja|桓武天皇|12=draft}}, the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=draft}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|12=draft}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific {{ill|Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|ja|官位相当|12=draft}}. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|12=draft}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=draft}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In {{ill|Kanpyō|ja|寛平|12=draft}} 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by {{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦|12=draft}} 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the {{ill|Gyōbushō|ja|刑部省|12=draft}} (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|12=draft}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=draft}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|12=draft}}'', which diverged from traditional {{ill|Ritsuryō law|ja|律令|12=draft}}. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''{{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当|12=draft}}'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|12=draft}}, a military organization of the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政|12=draft}} era. Under the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府|12=draft}}, the establishment of the {{ill|Rokuhara Tandai|ja|六波羅探題|12=draft}} further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, their authority was transferred to the {{ill|Samurai-dokoro|ja|侍所|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|12=draft}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|ja|貞和|12=draft}} era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=draft}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=draft}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; {{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当|12=draft}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=draft}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=draft}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=draft}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Daiyū|ja|大尉|12=draft}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=draft}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=draft}}. ; {{ill|Shōyū|ja|少尉|12=draft}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|12=draft}} or {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|12=draft}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=draft}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=draft}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=draft}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashōmon (short story)|ja|羅生門|12=draft}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|ja|藪の中|12=draft}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heian Kyō Alien|ja|平安京エイリアン|12=draft}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|12=draft}} * {{ill|押領使|ja|押領使|12=draft}}

revision.text:
[[ファイル:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|サムネイル|『[[伴大納言絵詞]]』に描かれた検非違使]] [[画像:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|サムネイル|[[知恩院]]を警護する検非違使]]
'''検非違使'''（けびいし、けんびいし）は[[日本]]の[[律令制]]下の[[令外官]]の役人。「（不法・違法など）非違を[[検察]]する（[[天皇]]の）使者」の意で、略して「非違」ともいう。検非違使庁の[[官人]]。佐と尉の[[唐名]]は[[廷尉]]。[[京都]]の治安維持と民政を所管した。[[平安時代]]初期に発足した。また、[[平安時代]]後期には[[令制国]]にも置かれるようになった。
== 概要 == [[平安時代]]の[[弘仁]]7年（816年）が初見で、その頃に設置されたと考えられている。当時の朝廷は、[[桓武天皇]]による[[軍団 (古代日本)|軍団]]の廃止以来、軍事力を事実上放棄していたが、その結果として、京都の治安が悪化したために、[[衛門府]]の組織を利用して創設し、[[衛門府]]の武官（中央官人）が[[宣旨]]によって検非違使を兼務し軍事・警察を担った。[[官位相当]]はない。五位から[[昇殿]]が許され[[殿上人]]となるため、[[武士]]の出世の目安となっていた。864年に東西の市司に事務所を置き、そこでは刑罰を行い見せしめとした。
[[寛平]]7年（[[895年]]）、左右衛門府内に左右の検非違使庁（役所）を置くようになったが、[[天暦]]元年（[[947年]]）に効率化、迅速化のために統合されて左庁だけに検非違使庁が置かれるようになった。
[[司法]]を担当していた[[刑部省]]、[[警察]]、[[監察]]を担当していた[[弾正台]]、[[首都|都]]に関わる[[行政]]、[[治安]]、[[司法]]を統括していた[[京職]]など他の[[官庁]]の職掌をだんだんと奪うようになり、検非違使は大きな権力を振るうようになった。
平安時代後期には[[刑事事件]]に関する職権行使のために[[律令]]とはちがった性質の「'''[[庁例]]'''」（使庁の流例ともいわれた慣習法）を適用するようになった。また、この頃から検非違使庁における事務は[[別当]]の自宅で行われるようになった。
平安時代末期になると[[院政]]の軍事組織である[[北面武士]]に取って代わられ、更に[[鎌倉幕府]]が[[六波羅探題]]を設置すると次第に弱体化し、[[室町時代]]には[[室町幕府|幕府]]が[[京都]]に置かれ、[[侍所]]に権限を掌握されることになった。もっとも、検非違使には、犯人の追捕を行う機能と、洛中の行政や刑事裁判を行う機能があり、前者は[[貞和]]年間から侍所に代替されるようになり、[[永徳]]3年（[[1383年]]）を最後に確認できなくなるが、後者はむしろ[[光厳院]]政期においては活発であり、[[後円融院]]政期の[[至徳 (日本)|至徳]]3年（[[1386年]]）ごろまで活動が確認できる。このことから[[森茂暁]]は、検非違使庁の衰滅時期を至徳末年としている<ref>森茂暁『南北朝期公武関係史の研究』1984年、pp.321-353。</ref>。
== 内部官職 == ;[[別当]] :[[四等官]]の長官（カミ）に相当する。[[唐名]]'''大理卿'''。定員は1名で、現任の正あるいは権中納言または参議にして、左あるいは右衛門督、または左あるいは右兵衛督の兼任者のいずれかより補任される慣例である。参議四位で検非違使別当を兼帯した例もある。一方、大納言以上の議政官で兼帯したのは、[[藤原忠平]]が911年（延喜11年）に大納言に転任するが、検非違使別当をそのまま兼帯した例のみで他の例はなく、五位以下から兼帯した例もない。なお、検非違使別当は検非違使を統轄する最高責任者ではあるが、自身は検非違使ではない。また、検非違使別当を兼帯した権中納言従三位兼行左衛門督[[柳原量光]]が1486年（文明18年）に辞職以降、1655年（明暦元年)10月26日、参議従三位行左衛門督の[[油小路隆貞]]が兼帯するまで兼帯者不在。また、途絶え、1744年（延享元年）8月29日、正三位行権中納言兼左衛門督[[柳原光綱]]が兼帯以降、明治維新まで後任者が継続する。ちなみに、史料における検非違使別当兼帯の初出は、834年（承和元年）1月27日、参議左大弁従四位上兼行左近衛中将春宮大夫武蔵守の[[文室秋津]]である。（公卿補任） ;[[佐]] :四等官の次官（スケ）に相当する。[[唐名]]'''[[廷尉]]'''。定員は2名で、左あるいは右衛門権佐が兼務していた。なお、原則として検非違使を務めるのは[[権官]]である左あるいは右衛門権佐であり、正官である左あるいは右衛門佐が検非違使を務めることはない。別当は兼務が多かったので実質的に検非違使庁の責任者であった。[[蔵人]]で検非違使佐を兼ねたり、蔵人・[[弁官]]・検非違使佐を兼ねる[[三事兼帯]]もいた。ちなみに、史料における検非違使佐兼帯の初出は、824年（天長元年）、左衛門権佐従五位上の[[笠仲守]]と従五位下守右衛門権佐の[[藤原永雄]]である。（帝王編年記） ;[[大尉]] :四等官の判官（ジョウ）に相当し、定員は4名で、衛門大尉が兼務していた。[[明法家]]である[[坂上氏]]及び[[中原氏]]が世襲するようになった。 ;[[少尉]] :四等官の判官（ジョウ）に相当し、定員は不定で、衛門尉が兼務していた。[[10世紀]]後半頃から[[源氏]]や[[平氏]]などの[[武士]]がなることが多かった。[[源義経]]を'''九郎判官'''と呼ぶのもこの官職に就いていたからである。 ;大志、少志 :四等官の主典（サカン）に相当する。定員は不定で、若年の明法家がなることが多かった。 ;府生 :他の官司における[[史生]]に相当する下級書記官。追捕や裁判に参加する。定員は変動したが、おおよそ2名から4名。 ;[[看督長]]（かどのおさ） :罪人を収監する[[監獄]]を管理する役であったが、後に罪人を捕縛する役になる。赤[[狩衣]]、白衣、布袴に白杖を持つ異形のいでたちで職務に当たった。 ;[[案主]]（あんじゅ） :検非違使庁の事務役人で、当初1名だったが後に人数が増えた。 ;[[火長]] :[[衛門府]]の[[衛士]]から選抜された者で、ここから看督長や案主が選ばれた。 ;[[放免]] :元罪人で、下部とも呼び、罪を許され検非違使庁で働くものである。実際に[[犯罪者]]を探索し、捕縛や[[拷問]]を担当した。 :
==検非違使が登場する作品== * 芥川龍之介「[[藪の中]]」では警察官のような立場として登場する。また、「[[羅生門 (小説)|羅生門]]」でも言及される。 * 「[[宇治拾遺物語]]」所収の『検非違使忠明のこと』（巻7第4話）は高等学校[[国語 (教科)|国語]]総合の教科書によく取り上げられる。同様の記述は「[[今昔物語集|今昔物語]]」にも見られる（巻19第40話）。 * 「今昔物語」には、他にも放免が強盗となった出来事が記されている。（巻29第6話「放免共、強盗ト為リ人ノ家ニ入リテ補ヘラルル語」） * 初期の[[コンピュータゲーム]]『[[平安京エイリアン]]』の[[プレイヤーズ・キャラクター|プレイヤーキャラクター]]は検非違使という設定であった。 * [[刀剣乱舞-ONLINE-]]（[[DMM.com]]・[[ニトロプラス]]）：プレイヤー側勢力、プレイヤーの敵勢力のどちらとも良しとしない第三勢力として登場。 * [[ニンジャスレイヤー]]：第二部:キョート・ヘル・オン・アースにおいて日本から独立した国家であるキョート共和国の治安維持要員として『ケビーシガード』が登場する。おそらく検非違使に由来するものと思われる。 *[[牙狼 -紅蓮ノ月-|牙狼-紅蓮ノ月-]]：劇場版 薄墨桜-GARO-：ネオ平安の世を取り締まる組織。最高権力者たる藤原道長に疑問を持つものも少なくなく、放免に愛情を抱き検非違使の座を捨て盗賊となった者もいる。劇場版では、検非違使庁すらも藤原道長の手にかかっているものとされた。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
==参考文献== {{参照方法|date=2013年10月|section=1}}
*[[小川清太郎]]「検非違使の研究（一）」 早稲田法学 17号 1939年9月発行 *小川清太郎「検非違使の研究（二・完）」 早稲田法学 18号 1939年11月発行 *[[井上満郎]]「検非違使の成立と摂関政治」 日本史研究 93号 1967年9月発行 *[[森田悌]]「検非違使の研究」 [[史学雑誌]] 78巻9号 1969年8月発行 *『京都府警察史 第1巻』京都府警察本部、1971年（平安時代から幕末まで、四「検非違使を中心とした平安京の治安」、121–190頁） *[[大饗亮]]「検非違使の研究1 制度史的考察）上)」 岡山大学法学会雑誌 20巻4号 1971年3月発行 *大饗亮「検非違使の研究1 制度史的考察（下）」 岡山大学法学会雑誌 21巻2号 1971年11月発行 *大饗亮「検非違使の研究2 平安時代の司法管轄権と検非違使」 岡山大学法学会雑誌 22巻1号 1972年10月発行 *[[駒井由美子]]「検非違使の成立に関する一考察」関西学院史学 20号 1981年5月発行 *[[笠原英彦]]「平安期検非違使小考」 [[法學研究]] 62巻3号 1989年 *[[百瀬今朝雄]]「中納言への道…参議大弁・検非違使別当」 [[立正史学]] 78号 1995年9月発行 *[[前田禎彦]]「検非違使別当と使庁：庁務の構造と変遷」 [[史林]] 82巻1号 1999年 *[[宮崎康充]]「鎌倉時代の検非違使」 [[書陵部]]紀要 51号 1999年 *前田禎彦「平安時代の法と秩序…検非違使庁の役割と意義」 [[日本史研究]] 452号 2000年4月発行 *[[笹山晴生]]「検非違使の成立」[[皇學館]]論叢 37巻3号 2004年6月発行 *[[中井裕子]]「検非違使別当の人事からみる鎌倉後期の朝廷」 日本史研究 528号 2006年8月発行 *中井裕子「建武新政期の検非違使庁について」 [[史泉]] 110号 2009年7月発行 *[[和田英松]]「官職要解」[[講談社学術文庫]] 1983年
==関連項目== *[[検非違使別当宣]] *[[神郡検非違使]] *[[押領使]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[検非違使]] to [[Office of Police and Justice]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
[[ファイル:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|サムネイル|『[[伴大納言絵詞]]』に描かれた検非違使]] [[画像:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|サムネイル|[[知恩院]]を警護する検非違使]]
'''検非違使'''（けびいし、けんびいし）は[[日本]]の[[律令制]]下の[[令外官]]の役人。「（不法・違法など）非違を[[検察]]する（[[天皇]]の）使者」の意で、略して「非違」ともいう。検非違使庁の[[官人]]。佐と尉の[[唐名]]は[[廷尉]]。[[京都]]の治安維持と民政を所管した。[[平安時代]]初期に発足した。また、[[平安時代]]後期には[[令制国]]にも置かれるようになった。
== 概要 == [[平安時代]]の[[弘仁]]7年（816年）が初見で、その頃に設置されたと考えられている。当時の朝廷は、[[桓武天皇]]による[[軍団 (古代日本)|軍団]]の廃止以来、軍事力を事実上放棄していたが、その結果として、京都の治安が悪化したために、[[衛門府]]の組織を利用して創設し、[[衛門府]]の武官（中央官人）が[[宣旨]]によって検非違使を兼務し軍事・警察を担った。[[官位相当]]はない。五位から[[昇殿]]が許され[[殿上人]]となるため、[[武士]]の出世の目安となっていた。864年に東西の市司に事務所を置き、そこでは刑罰を行い見せしめとした。
[[寛平]]7年（[[895年]]）、左右衛門府内に左右の検非違使庁（役所）を置くようになったが、[[天暦]]元年（[[947年]]）に効率化、迅速化のために統合されて左庁だけに検非違使庁が置かれるようになった。
[[司法]]を担当していた[[刑部省]]、[[警察]]、[[監察]]を担当していた[[弾正台]]、[[首都|都]]に関わる[[行政]]、[[治安]]、[[司法]]を統括していた[[京職]]など他の[[官庁]]の職掌をだんだんと奪うようになり、検非違使は大きな権力を振るうようになった。
平安時代後期には[[刑事事件]]に関する職権行使のために[[律令]]とはちがった性質の「'''[[庁例]]'''」（使庁の流例ともいわれた慣習法）を適用するようになった。また、この頃から検非違使庁における事務は[[別当]]の自宅で行われるようになった。
平安時代末期になると[[院政]]の軍事組織である[[北面武士]]に取って代わられ、更に[[鎌倉幕府]]が[[六波羅探題]]を設置すると次第に弱体化し、[[室町時代]]には[[室町幕府|幕府]]が[[京都]]に置かれ、[[侍所]]に権限を掌握されることになった。もっとも、検非違使には、犯人の追捕を行う機能と、洛中の行政や刑事裁判を行う機能があり、前者は[[貞和]]年間から侍所に代替されるようになり、[[永徳]]3年（[[1383年]]）を最後に確認できなくなるが、後者はむしろ[[光厳院]]政期においては活発であり、[[後円融院]]政期の[[至徳 (日本)|至徳]]3年（[[1386年]]）ごろまで活動が確認できる。このことから[[森茂暁]]は、検非違使庁の衰滅時期を至徳末年としている<ref>森茂暁『南北朝期公武関係史の研究』1984年、pp.321-353。</ref>。
== 内部官職 == ;[[別当]] :[[四等官]]の長官（カミ）に相当する。[[唐名]]'''大理卿'''。定員は1名で、現任の正あるいは権中納言または参議にして、左あるいは右衛門督、または左あるいは右兵衛督の兼任者のいずれかより補任される慣例である。参議四位で検非違使別当を兼帯した例もある。一方、大納言以上の議政官で兼帯したのは、[[藤原忠平]]が911年（延喜11年）に大納言に転任するが、検非違使別当をそのまま兼帯した例のみで他の例はなく、五位以下から兼帯した例もない。なお、検非違使別当は検非違使を統轄する最高責任者ではあるが、自身は検非違使ではない。また、検非違使別当を兼帯した権中納言従三位兼行左衛門督[[柳原量光]]が1486年（文明18年）に辞職以降、1655年（明暦元年)10月26日、参議従三位行左衛門督の[[油小路隆貞]]が兼帯するまで兼帯者不在。また、途絶え、1744年（延享元年）8月29日、正三位行権中納言兼左衛門督[[柳原光綱]]が兼帯以降、明治維新まで後任者が継続する。ちなみに、史料における検非違使別当兼帯の初出は、834年（承和元年）1月27日、参議左大弁従四位上兼行左近衛中将春宮大夫武蔵守の[[文室秋津]]である。（公卿補任） ;[[佐]] :四等官の次官（スケ）に相当する。[[唐名]]'''[[廷尉]]'''。定員は2名で、左あるいは右衛門権佐が兼務していた。なお、原則として検非違使を務めるのは[[権官]]である左あるいは右衛門権佐であり、正官である左あるいは右衛門佐が検非違使を務めることはない。別当は兼務が多かったので実質的に検非違使庁の責任者であった。[[蔵人]]で検非違使佐を兼ねたり、蔵人・[[弁官]]・検非違使佐を兼ねる[[三事兼帯]]もいた。ちなみに、史料における検非違使佐兼帯の初出は、824年（天長元年）、左衛門権佐従五位上の[[笠仲守]]と従五位下守右衛門権佐の[[藤原永雄]]である。（帝王編年記） ;[[大尉]] :四等官の判官（ジョウ）に相当し、定員は4名で、衛門大尉が兼務していた。[[明法家]]である[[坂上氏]]及び[[中原氏]]が世襲するようになった。 ;[[少尉]] :四等官の判官（ジョウ）に相当し、定員は不定で、衛門尉が兼務していた。[[10世紀]]後半頃から[[源氏]]や[[平氏]]などの[[武士]]がなることが多かった。[[源義経]]を'''九郎判官'''と呼ぶのもこの官職に就いていたからである。 ;大志、少志 :四等官の主典（サカン）に相当する。定員は不定で、若年の明法家がなることが多かった。 ;府生 :他の官司における[[史生]]に相当する下級書記官。追捕や裁判に参加する。定員は変動したが、おおよそ2名から4名。 ;[[看督長]]（かどのおさ） :罪人を収監する[[監獄]]を管理する役であったが、後に罪人を捕縛する役になる。赤[[狩衣]]、白衣、布袴に白杖を持つ異形のいでたちで職務に当たった。 ;[[案主]]（あんじゅ） :検非違使庁の事務役人で、当初1名だったが後に人数が増えた。 ;[[火長]] :[[衛門府]]の[[衛士]]から選抜された者で、ここから看督長や案主が選ばれた。 ;[[放免]] :元罪人で、下部とも呼び、罪を許され検非違使庁で働くものである。実際に[[犯罪者]]を探索し、捕縛や[[拷問]]を担当した。 :
==検非違使が登場する作品== * 芥川龍之介「[[藪の中]]」では警察官のような立場として登場する。また、「[[羅生門 (小説)|羅生門]]」でも言及される。 * 「[[宇治拾遺物語]]」所収の『検非違使忠明のこと』（巻7第4話）は高等学校[[国語 (教科)|国語]]総合の教科書によく取り上げられる。同様の記述は「[[今昔物語集|今昔物語]]」にも見られる（巻19第40話）。 * 「今昔物語」には、他にも放免が強盗となった出来事が記されている。（巻29第6話「放免共、強盗ト為リ人ノ家ニ入リテ補ヘラルル語」） * 初期の[[コンピュータゲーム]]『[[平安京エイリアン]]』の[[プレイヤーズ・キャラクター|プレイヤーキャラクター]]は検非違使という設定であった。 * [[刀剣乱舞-ONLINE-]]（[[DMM.com]]・[[ニトロプラス]]）：プレイヤー側勢力、プレイヤーの敵勢力のどちらとも良しとしない第三勢力として登場。 * [[ニンジャスレイヤー]]：第二部:キョート・ヘル・オン・アースにおいて日本から独立した国家であるキョート共和国の治安維持要員として『ケビーシガード』が登場する。おそらく検非違使に由来するものと思われる。 *[[牙狼 -紅蓮ノ月-|牙狼-紅蓮ノ月-]]：劇場版 薄墨桜-GARO-：ネオ平安の世を取り締まる組織。最高権力者たる藤原道長に疑問を持つものも少なくなく、放免に愛情を抱き検非違使の座を捨て盗賊となった者もいる。劇場版では、検非違使庁すらも藤原道長の手にかかっているものとされた。

[[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''{{ill|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|ja|伴大納言絵詞|12=draft|12=draft}}'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding {{ill|Chion-in|ja|知恩院|12=draft|12=draft}}]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制|12=draft|12=draft}} officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|12=draft|12=draft}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft|12=draft}} and were first established in the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}}. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁|12=draft|12=draft}} 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|12=draft|12=draft}} were abolished under {{ill|Emperor Kanmu|ja|桓武天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}, the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=draft|12=draft}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|12=draft|12=draft}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific {{ill|Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|ja|官位相当|1=Kan'i sōtō system|2=ja|3=官位相当制|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|12=draft|12=draft}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=draft|12=draft}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In {{ill|Kanpyō|ja|寛平|1=Kanpyō (era)|lt=Kanpyō|qq= (era)|1=Kanpyō (era)|lt=Kanpyō|qq= (era)|12=draft|12=draft}} 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by {{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦|12=draft|12=draft}} 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the {{ill|Gyōbushō|ja|刑部省|1=Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of Justice|qq= (pre-modern Japan)|1=Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of Justice|12=draft|12=draft}} (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|12=draft|12=draft}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=draft|12=draft}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|12=draft|12=draft}}'', which diverged from traditional {{ill|Ritsuryō law|ja|律令|12=draft|12=draft}}. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''{{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当|12=draft|12=draft}}'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|12=draft|12=draft}}, a military organization of the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政|12=draft|12=draft}} era. Under the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府|12=draft|12=draft}}, the establishment of the {{ill|Rokuhara Tandai|ja|六波羅探題|12=draft|12=draft}} further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, their authority was transferred to the {{ill|Samurai-dokoro|ja|侍所|12=draft|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|ja|貞和|12=draft|12=draft}} era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=draft|12=draft}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=draft|12=draft}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; {{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当|12=draft|12=draft}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=draft|12=draft}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=draft|12=draft}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=draft|12=draft}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Daiyū|ja|大尉|12=draft|12=draft}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=draft|12=draft}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=draft|12=draft}}. ; {{ill|Shōyū|ja|少尉|12=draft|12=draft}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|12=draft|12=draft}} or {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|12=draft|12=draft}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=draft|12=draft}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=draft|12=draft}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=draft|12=draft}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashōmon (short story)|ja|羅生門}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|ja|藪の中|12=draft|12=draft}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heian Kyō Alien|ja|平安京エイリアン|12=draft|12=draft}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.

== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|押領使|ja|押領使|12=draft|12=draft}}

; {{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Daiyū|ja|大尉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. ; {{ill|Shōyū|ja|少尉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashōmon (short story)|ja|羅生門}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|ja|藪の中|qq=draft|qq=draft}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heian Kyō Alien|ja|平安京エイリアン|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|押領使|ja|押領使|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

revision.text:
[[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''{{ill|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|ja|伴大納言絵詞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|qq=}}'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding {{ill|Chion-in|ja|知恩院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chion-in|qq=}}]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}} officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|qq=}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} and were first established in the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin|qq=}} 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|qq=}} were abolished under {{ill|Emperor Kanmu|ja|桓武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kanmu|qq=}}, the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific {{ill|Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|ja|官位相当|1=Kan'i sōtō system|2=ja|3=官位相当制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan'i sōtō system|qq=}}. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|qq=}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In {{ill|Kanpyō|ja|寛平|1=Kanpyō (era)|lt=Kanpyō|qq= (era)|1=Kanpyō (era)|lt=Kanpyō|qq= (era)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanpyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanpyō (era)|qq=}} 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by {{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryaku|qq=}} 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the {{ill|Gyōbushō|ja|刑部省|1=Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of Justice|qq= (pre-modern Japan)|1=Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of Justice|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōbushō|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|qq=}} (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|qq=}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|qq=}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|qq=}}'', which diverged from traditional {{ill|Ritsuryō law|ja|律令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō law|qq=}}. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''{{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō (Japan)|qq=}}'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|qq=}}, a military organization of the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Insei|qq=}} era. Under the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura shogunate|qq=}}, the establishment of the {{ill|Rokuhara Tandai|ja|六波羅探題|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuhara Tandai|qq=}} further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, their authority was transferred to the {{ill|Samurai-dokoro|ja|侍所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai-dokoro|qq=}} of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|ja|貞和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōwa (Muromachi period)|qq=}} era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|qq=}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|qq=}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; {{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō (Japan)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Daiyū|ja|大尉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daiyū|qq=}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|qq=}}. ; {{ill|Shōyū|ja|少尉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōyū|qq=}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan|qq=}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|qq=}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|qq=}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|qq=}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|qq=}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashōmon (short story)|ja|羅生門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rashōmon (short story)|qq=}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|ja|藪の中|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/In a Grove|qq=}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heian Kyō Alien|ja|平安京エイリアン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Kyō Alien|qq=}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi Bettō-sen|qq=}} * {{ill|押領使|ja|押領使|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/押領使|qq=}}

; {{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō (Japan)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Daiyū|ja|大尉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daiyū|qq=}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|qq=}}. ; {{ill|Shōyū|ja|少尉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōyū|qq=}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan|qq=}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|qq=}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|qq=}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|qq=}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|qq=}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashōmon (short story)|ja|羅生門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rashōmon (short story)|qq=}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|ja|藪の中|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/In a Grove|qq=}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heian Kyō Alien|ja|平安京エイリアン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Kyō Alien|qq=}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi Bettō-sen|qq=}} * {{ill|押領使|ja|押領使|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/押領使|qq=}}

[[sn:Office of Police and Justice]] [[de:Kebiishi]] [[it:Kebiishi]] [[ko:검비위사]] [[zh:檢非違使]] [[fa:کبیشی]] [[ja:検非違使]] [[en:Office of Police and Justice]]

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Office of Police and Justice]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''{{ill|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|ja|伴大納言絵詞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|qq=}}'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding {{ill|Chion-in|ja|知恩院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chion-in|qq=}}]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}} officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|qq=}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} and were first established in the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin|qq=}} 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|qq=}} were abolished under {{ill|Emperor Kanmu|ja|桓武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kanmu|qq=}}, the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific {{ill|Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|ja|官位相当|1=Kan'i sōtō system|2=ja|3=官位相当制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan'i sōtō system|qq=}}. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|qq=}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In {{ill|Kanpyō|ja|寛平|1=Kanpyō (era)|lt=Kanpyō|qq= (era)|1=Kanpyō (era)|lt=Kanpyō|qq= (era)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanpyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanpyō (era)|qq=}} 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by {{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryaku|qq=}} 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the {{ill|Gyōbushō|ja|刑部省|1=Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of Justice|qq= (pre-modern Japan)|1=Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of Justice|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōbushō|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|qq=}} (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|qq=}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|qq=}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|qq=}}'', which diverged from traditional {{ill|Ritsuryō law|ja|律令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō law|qq=}}. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''{{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō (Japan)|qq=}}'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|qq=}}, a military organization of the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Insei|qq=}} era. Under the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura shogunate|qq=}}, the establishment of the {{ill|Rokuhara Tandai|ja|六波羅探題|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuhara Tandai|qq=}} further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, their authority was transferred to the {{ill|Samurai-dokoro|ja|侍所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai-dokoro|qq=}} of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|ja|貞和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōwa (Muromachi period)|qq=}} era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|qq=}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|qq=}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; {{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō (Japan)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Daiyū|ja|大尉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daiyū|qq=}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|qq=}}. ; {{ill|Shōyū|ja|少尉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōyū|qq=}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan|qq=}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|qq=}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|qq=}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|qq=}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|qq=}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashōmon (short story)|ja|羅生門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rashōmon (short story)|qq=}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|ja|藪の中|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/In a Grove|qq=}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heian Kyō Alien|ja|平安京エイリアン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Kyō Alien|qq=}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi Bettō-sen|qq=}} * {{ill|押領使|ja|押領使|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/押領使|qq=}}

== Internal Positions ==
; {{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō (Japan)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Daiyū|ja|大尉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daiyū|qq=}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|qq=}}. ; {{ill|Shōyū|ja|少尉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōyū|qq=}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan|qq=}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|qq=}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|qq=}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|qq=}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|qq=}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashōmon (short story)|ja|羅生門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rashōmon (short story)|qq=}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|ja|藪の中|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/In a Grove|qq=}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heian Kyō Alien|ja|平安京エイリアン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Kyō Alien|qq=}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi Bettō-sen|qq=}} * {{ill|押領使|ja|押領使|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/押領使|qq=}}
