[[sn:Office of Police and Justice]] [[de:Kebiishi]] [[it:Kebiishi]] [[ko:검비위사]] [[zh:檢非違使]] [[fa:کبیشی]] [[ja:検非違使]] [[en:Office of Police and Justice]]

[[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''{{ill|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|ja|伴大納言絵詞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|qq=}}'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding {{ill|Chion-in|ja|知恩院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chion-in|qq=}}]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}} officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|qq=}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} and were first established in the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin|qq=}} 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|qq=}} were abolished under {{ill|Emperor Kanmu|ja|桓武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kanmu|qq=}}, the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific {{ill|Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|ja|官位相当|1=Kan'i sōtō system|2=ja|3=官位相当制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan'i sōtō system|qq=}}. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|qq=}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In {{ill|Kanpyō|ja|寛平|1=Kanpyō (era)|lt=Kanpyō|qq= (era)|1=Kanpyō (era)|lt=Kanpyō|qq= (era)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanpyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanpyō (era)|qq=}} 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by {{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryaku|qq=}} 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the {{ill|Gyōbushō|ja|刑部省|1=Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of Justice|qq= (pre-modern Japan)|1=Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of Justice|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōbushō|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|qq=}} (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|qq=}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|qq=}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|qq=}}'', which diverged from traditional {{ill|Ritsuryō law|ja|律令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō law|qq=}}. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''{{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō (Japan)|qq=}}'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|qq=}}, a military organization of the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Insei|qq=}} era. Under the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura shogunate|qq=}}, the establishment of the {{ill|Rokuhara Tandai|ja|六波羅探題|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuhara Tandai|qq=}} further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, their authority was transferred to the {{ill|Samurai-dokoro|ja|侍所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai-dokoro|qq=}} of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|ja|貞和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōwa (Muromachi period)|qq=}} era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|qq=}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|qq=}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; {{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō (Japan)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Daiyū|ja|大尉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daiyū|qq=}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|qq=}}. ; {{ill|Shōyū|ja|少尉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōyū|qq=}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan|qq=}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|qq=}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|qq=}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|qq=}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|qq=}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashōmon (short story)|ja|羅生門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rashōmon (short story)|qq=}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|ja|藪の中|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/In a Grove|qq=}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heian Kyō Alien|ja|平安京エイリアン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Kyō Alien|qq=}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.

; {{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō (Japan)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Daiyū|ja|大尉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daiyū|qq=}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|qq=}}. ; {{ill|Shōyū|ja|少尉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōyū|qq=}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan|qq=}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|qq=}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|qq=}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|qq=}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|qq=}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashōmon (short story)|ja|羅生門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rashōmon (short story)|qq=}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|ja|藪の中|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/In a Grove|qq=}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heian Kyō Alien|ja|平安京エイリアン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Kyō Alien|qq=}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.

[[sn:Office of Police and Justice]] [[de:Kebiishi]] [[it:Kebiishi]] [[ko:검비위사]] [[zh:檢非違使]] [[fa:کبیشی]] [[ja:検非違使]] [[en:Office of Police and Justice]]

[[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''{{ill|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|ja|伴大納言絵詞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|qq=}}'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding {{ill|Chion-in|ja|知恩院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chion-in|qq=}}]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}} officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|qq=}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} and were first established in the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin|qq=}} 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|qq=}} were abolished under {{ill|Emperor Kanmu|ja|桓武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kanmu|qq=}}, the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific {{ill|Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|ja|官位相当|1=Kan'i sōtō system|2=ja|3=官位相当制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan'i sōtō system|qq=}}. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|qq=}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In {{ill|Kanpyō|ja|寛平|1=Kanpyō (era)|lt=Kanpyō|qq= (era)|1=Kanpyō (era)|lt=Kanpyō|qq= (era)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanpyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanpyō (era)|qq=}} 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by {{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryaku|qq=}} 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the {{ill|Gyōbushō|ja|刑部省|1=Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of Justice|qq= (pre-modern Japan)|1=Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of Justice|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōbushō|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|qq=}} (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|qq=}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|qq=}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|qq=}}'', which diverged from traditional {{ill|Ritsuryō law|ja|律令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō law|qq=}}. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''{{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō (Japan)|qq=}}'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|qq=}}, a military organization of the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Insei|qq=}} era. Under the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura shogunate|qq=}}, the establishment of the {{ill|Rokuhara Tandai|ja|六波羅探題|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuhara Tandai|qq=}} further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, their authority was transferred to the {{ill|Samurai-dokoro|ja|侍所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai-dokoro|qq=}} of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|ja|貞和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōwa (Muromachi period)|qq=}} era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|qq=}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|qq=}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>

The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Daiyū|ja|大尉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daiyū|qq=}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|qq=}}. ; {{ill|Shōyū|ja|少尉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōyū|qq=}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan|qq=}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|qq=}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|qq=}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|qq=}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|qq=}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.

The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Daiyū|ja|大尉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daiyū|qq=}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|qq=}}. ; {{ill|Shōyū|ja|少尉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōyū|qq=}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan|qq=}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|qq=}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|qq=}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|qq=}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|qq=}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashōmon (short story)|ja|羅生門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rashōmon (short story)|qq=}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|ja|藪の中|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/In a Grove|qq=}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heian Kyō Alien|ja|平安京エイリアン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Kyō Alien|qq=}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi Bettō-sen|qq=}} * {{ill|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|ja|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/押領使|qq=}}

revision.text:
[[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''{{ill|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|ja|伴大納言絵詞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|qq=}}'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding {{ill|Chion-in|ja|知恩院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chion-in|qq=}}]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}} officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|qq=}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} and were first established in the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from {{ill|Kōnin|ja|弘仁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin|qq=}} 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|qq=}} were abolished under {{ill|Emperor Kanmu|ja|桓武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kanmu|qq=}}, the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific {{ill|Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|ja|官位相当|1=Kan'i sōtō system|2=ja|3=官位相当制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan'i sōtō system|qq=}}. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|qq=}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In {{ill|Kanpyō|ja|寛平|1=Kanpyō (era)|lt=Kanpyō|qq= (era)|1=Kanpyō (era)|lt=Kanpyō|qq= (era)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanpyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanpyō (era)|qq=}} 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by {{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenryaku|qq=}} 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the {{ill|Gyōbushō|ja|刑部省|1=Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of Justice|qq= (pre-modern Japan)|1=Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of Justice|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōbushō|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|qq=}} (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|qq=}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|qq=}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|qq=}}'', which diverged from traditional {{ill|Ritsuryō law|ja|律令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō law|qq=}}. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''{{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō (Japan)|qq=}}'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|qq=}}, a military organization of the {{ill|Insei|ja|院政|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Insei|qq=}} era. Under the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura shogunate|qq=}}, the establishment of the {{ill|Rokuhara Tandai|ja|六波羅探題|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuhara Tandai|qq=}} further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, their authority was transferred to the {{ill|Samurai-dokoro|ja|侍所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai-dokoro|qq=}} of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|ja|貞和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōwa (Muromachi period)|qq=}} era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|qq=}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|qq=}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; {{ill|Bettō (Japan)|ja|別当|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō (Japan)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Daiyū|ja|大尉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daiyū|qq=}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|qq=}}. ; {{ill|Shōyū|ja|少尉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōyū|qq=}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan|qq=}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|qq=}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|qq=}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|qq=}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|qq=}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashōmon (short story)|ja|羅生門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rashōmon (short story)|qq=}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|ja|藪の中|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/In a Grove|qq=}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heian Kyō Alien|ja|平安京エイリアン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Kyō Alien|qq=}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi Bettō-sen|qq=}} * {{ill|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|ja|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/押領使|qq=}}

The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Daiyū|ja|大尉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daiyū|qq=}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|qq=}}. ; {{ill|Shōyū|ja|少尉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōyū|qq=}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan|qq=}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|qq=}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|qq=}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|qq=}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|qq=}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashōmon (short story)|ja|羅生門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rashōmon (short story)|qq=}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|ja|藪の中|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/In a Grove|qq=}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heian Kyō Alien|ja|平安京エイリアン|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Kyō Alien|qq=}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.

revision.comment: Bot: replace {{ill}} with enwiki link via Wikidata
revision.text:
[[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''[[:en:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba]]'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|qq=}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} and were first established in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]] 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|qq=}} were abolished under [[:en:Emperor Kanmu|Emperor Kanmu]], the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific {{ill|Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|ja|官位相当|1=Kan'i sōtō system|2=ja|3=官位相当制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan'i sōtō system|qq=}}. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|qq=}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In [[:en:Kanpyō (era)|Kanpyō]] 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|qq=}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|qq=}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|qq=}}'', which diverged from traditional [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō law]]. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]]'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|qq=}}, a military organization of the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] era. Under the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the establishment of the [[:en:Rokuhara Tandai|Rokuhara Tandai]] further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], their authority was transferred to the [[:en:Samurai-dokoro|Samurai-dokoro]] of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa (Muromachi period)]] era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|qq=}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|qq=}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|qq=}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|qq=}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|qq=}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|qq=}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|qq=}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "[[:en:Rashomon (disambiguation)|Rashōmon (short story)]]" and "[[:en:In a Grove|In a Grove]]" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''[[:en:Heiankyo Alien|Heian Kyō Alien]]'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi Bettō-sen|qq=}} * {{ill|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|ja|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/押領使|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|検非違使|version=103152179|comment=Imported full ja history}}

They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific {{ill|Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|ja|官位相当|1=Kan'i sōtō system|2=ja|3=官位相当制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan'i sōtō system|qq=}}. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|qq=}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In [[:en:Kanpyō (era)|Kanpyō]] 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|qq=}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|qq=}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|qq=}}'', which diverged from traditional [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō law]]. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]]'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|qq=}}, a military organization of the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] era. Under the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the establishment of the [[:en:Rokuhara Tandai|Rokuhara Tandai]] further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], their authority was transferred to the [[:en:Samurai-dokoro|Samurai-dokoro]] of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa (Muromachi period)]] era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|qq=}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|qq=}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|qq=}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|qq=}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|qq=}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|qq=}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|qq=}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "[[:en:Rashomon (disambiguation)|Rashōmon (short story)]]" and "[[:en:In a Grove|In a Grove]]" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''[[:en:Heiankyo Alien|Heian Kyō Alien]]'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi Bettō-sen|qq=}} * {{ill|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|ja|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/押領使|qq=}}

[[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''[[:en:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba]]'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|qq=}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} and were first established in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]] 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|qq=}} were abolished under [[:en:Emperor Kanmu|Emperor Kanmu]], the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific {{ill|Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|ja|官位相当|1=Kan'i sōtō system|2=ja|3=官位相当制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan'i sōtō system|qq=}}. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|qq=}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In [[:en:Kanpyō (era)|Kanpyō]] 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|qq=}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|qq=}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|qq=}}'', which diverged from traditional [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō law]]. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]]'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|qq=}}, a military organization of the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] era. Under the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the establishment of the [[:en:Rokuhara Tandai|Rokuhara Tandai]] further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], their authority was transferred to the [[:en:Samurai-dokoro|Samurai-dokoro]] of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa (Muromachi period)]] era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|qq=}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|qq=}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|qq=}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|qq=}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|qq=}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|qq=}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|qq=}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "[[:en:Rashomon (disambiguation)|Rashōmon (short story)]]" and "[[:en:In a Grove|In a Grove]]" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''[[:en:Heiankyo Alien|Heian Kyō Alien]]'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:History of Japan
revision.text:
[[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''[[:en:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba]]'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|qq=}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} and were first established in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]] 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|qq=}} were abolished under [[:en:Emperor Kanmu|Emperor Kanmu]], the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific {{ill|Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|ja|官位相当|1=Kan'i sōtō system|2=ja|3=官位相当制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan'i sōtō system|qq=}}. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|qq=}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In [[:en:Kanpyō (era)|Kanpyō]] 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|qq=}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|qq=}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|qq=}}'', which diverged from traditional [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō law]]. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]]'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|qq=}}, a military organization of the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] era. Under the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the establishment of the [[:en:Rokuhara Tandai|Rokuhara Tandai]] further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], their authority was transferred to the [[:en:Samurai-dokoro|Samurai-dokoro]] of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa (Muromachi period)]] era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|qq=}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|qq=}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|qq=}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|qq=}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|qq=}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|qq=}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|qq=}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "[[:en:Rashomon (disambiguation)|Rashōmon (short story)]]" and "[[:en:In a Grove|In a Grove]]" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''[[:en:Heiankyo Alien|Heian Kyō Alien]]'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi Bettō-sen|qq=}} * {{ill|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|ja|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/押領使|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|検非違使|version=103152179|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa (Muromachi period)]] era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|qq=}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|qq=}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|qq=}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|qq=}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|qq=}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|qq=}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|qq=}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "[[:en:Rashomon (disambiguation)|Rashōmon (short story)]]" and "[[:en:In a Grove|In a Grove]]" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''[[:en:Heiankyo Alien|Heian Kyō Alien]]'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi Bettō-sen|qq=}} * {{ill|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|ja|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/押領使|qq=}}

revision.text:
[[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''[[:en:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba]]'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|qq=}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} and were first established in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]] 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|qq=}} were abolished under [[:en:Emperor Kanmu|Emperor Kanmu]], the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific {{ill|Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|ja|官位相当|1=Kan'i sōtō system|2=ja|3=官位相当制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rank equivalence (Ritsuryō)|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan'i sōtō system|qq=}}. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|qq=}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In [[:en:Kanpyō (era)|Kanpyō]] 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|qq=}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|qq=}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|qq=}}'', which diverged from traditional [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō law]]. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]]'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|qq=}}, a military organization of the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] era. Under the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the establishment of the [[:en:Rokuhara Tandai|Rokuhara Tandai]] further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], their authority was transferred to the [[:en:Samurai-dokoro|Samurai-dokoro]] of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa (Muromachi period)]] era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|qq=}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|qq=}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|qq=}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|qq=}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|qq=}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|qq=}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|qq=}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|qq=}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|qq=}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "[[:en:Rashomon (disambiguation)|Rashōmon (short story)]]" and "[[:en:In a Grove|In a Grove]]" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''[[:en:Heiankyo Alien|Heian Kyō Alien]]'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi Bettō-sen|qq=}} * {{ill|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|ja|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/押領使|qq=}}
