The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|qq=|1=Danjōdai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487784}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|qq=|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|qq=|1=Chōrei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11484277}}'', which diverged from traditional [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō law]]. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]]'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|qq=|1=Hokumen no Bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404509}}, a military organization of the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] era. Under the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the establishment of the [[:en:Rokuhara Tandai|Rokuhara Tandai]] further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], their authority was transferred to the [[:en:Samurai-dokoro|Samurai-dokoro]] of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa (Muromachi period)]] era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至徳|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|qq=|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|qq=|1=Sa (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|qq=|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|qq=|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|qq=|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|qq=|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kebiishi]][[fa:کبیشی]][[it:Kebiishi]][[ja:検非違使]][[ko:게비이시]][[zh:檢非違使]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q497217]] Wikidata name: Kebiishi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''[[:en:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba]]'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|1=East Asian extra-statutory office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378315}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and were first established in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]] 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|1=militia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637390}} were abolished under [[:en:Emperor Kanmu|Emperor Kanmu]], the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]]. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|1=court nobles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10369708}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In [[:en:Kanpyō (era)|Kanpyō]] 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|1=Danjōdai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487784}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|1=Chōrei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11484277}}'', which diverged from traditional [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō law]]. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]]'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|1=Hokumen no Bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404509}}, a military organization of the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] era. Under the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the establishment of the [[:en:Rokuhara Tandai|Rokuhara Tandai]] further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], their authority was transferred to the [[:en:Samurai-dokoro|Samurai-dokoro]] of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa (Muromachi period)]] era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至徳|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Sa (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.

Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至徳|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Sa (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "[[:en:Rashomon (disambiguation)|Rashōmon (short story)]]" and "[[:en:In a Grove|In a Grove]]" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''[[:en:Heiankyo Alien|Heian Kyō Alien]]'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.

== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当宣|1=bettō-sen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396753}} * {{ill|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|ja|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/押領使|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497733}}

[[Category:Extraordinary Government Officials of Ancient Japan]] [[Category:Criminal Procedure Law in Japan]] [[Category:Police history of Japan]] [[Category:Public security systems]] [[Category:History of Japan]] [[Category:National law enforcement agencies of Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:게비이시]] <!--dewiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:게비이시]] <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:게비이시]] {{wikidata link|Q497217}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kebiishi]][[fa:کبیشی]][[it:Kebiishi]][[ja:検非違使]][[ko:게비이시]][[zh:檢非違使]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q497217]] Wikidata name: Kebiishi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''[[:en:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba]]'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|1=East Asian extra-statutory office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378315}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and were first established in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]] 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|1=militia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637390}} were abolished under [[:en:Emperor Kanmu|Emperor Kanmu]], the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]]. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|1=court nobles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10369708}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In [[:en:Kanpyō (era)|Kanpyō]] 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|1=Danjōdai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487784}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|1=Chōrei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11484277}}'', which diverged from traditional [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō law]]. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]]'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|1=Hokumen no Bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404509}}, a military organization of the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] era. Under the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the establishment of the [[:en:Rokuhara Tandai|Rokuhara Tandai]] further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], their authority was transferred to the [[:en:Samurai-dokoro|Samurai-dokoro]] of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa (Muromachi period)]] era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至徳|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Sa (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "[[:en:Rashomon (disambiguation)|Rashōmon (short story)]]" and "[[:en:In a Grove|In a Grove]]" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''[[:en:Heiankyo Alien|Heian Kyō Alien]]'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kebiishi]][[fa:کبیشی]][[it:Kebiishi]][[ja:検非違使]][[ko:게비이시]][[zh:檢非違使]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q497217]] Wikidata name: Kebiishi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''[[:en:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba]]'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|1=East Asian extra-statutory office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378315}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and were first established in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]] 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|1=militia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637390}} were abolished under [[:en:Emperor Kanmu|Emperor Kanmu]], the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]]. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|1=court nobles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10369708}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In [[:en:Kanpyō (era)|Kanpyō]] 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|1=Danjōdai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487784}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|1=Chōrei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11484277}}'', which diverged from traditional [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō law]]. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]]'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|1=Hokumen no Bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404509}}, a military organization of the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] era. Under the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the establishment of the [[:en:Rokuhara Tandai|Rokuhara Tandai]] further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], their authority was transferred to the [[:en:Samurai-dokoro|Samurai-dokoro]] of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa (Muromachi period)]] era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至徳|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Sa (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "[[:en:Rashomon (disambiguation)|Rashōmon (short story)]]" and "[[:en:In a Grove|In a Grove]]" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''[[:en:Heiankyo Alien|Heian Kyō Alien]]'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}

== Internal Positions ==
; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Sa (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "[[:en:Rashomon (disambiguation)|Rashōmon (short story)]]" and "[[:en:In a Grove|In a Grove]]" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''[[:en:Heiankyo Alien|Heian Kyō Alien]]'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.

'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|1=East Asian extra-statutory office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378315}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and were first established in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]] 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|1=militia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637390}} were abolished under [[:en:Emperor Kanmu|Emperor Kanmu]], the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]]. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|1=court nobles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10369708}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In [[:en:Kanpyō (era)|Kanpyō]] 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|1=Danjōdai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487784}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|1=Chōrei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11484277}}'', which diverged from traditional [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō law]]. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]]'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|1=Hokumen no Bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404509}}, a military organization of the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] era. Under the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the establishment of the [[:en:Rokuhara Tandai|Rokuhara Tandai]] further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], their authority was transferred to the [[:en:Samurai-dokoro|Samurai-dokoro]] of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa (Muromachi period)]] era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至徳|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Sa (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "[[:en:Rashomon (disambiguation)|Rashōmon (short story)]]" and "[[:en:In a Grove|In a Grove]]" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''[[:en:Heiankyo Alien|Heian Kyō Alien]]'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.

'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|1=East Asian extra-statutory office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378315}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and were first established in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.

However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|1=court nobles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10369708}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}}.

During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]]'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|1=Hokumen no Bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404509}}, a military organization of the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] era. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], their authority was transferred to the [[:en:Samurai-dokoro|Samurai-dokoro]] of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.

Under the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the establishment of the [[:en:Rokuhara Tandai|Rokuhara Tandai]] further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], their authority was transferred to the [[:en:Samurai-dokoro|Samurai-dokoro]] of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa (Muromachi period)]] era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至徳|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Sa (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "[[:en:Rashomon (disambiguation)|Rashōmon (short story)]]" and "[[:en:In a Grove|In a Grove]]" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''[[:en:Heiankyo Alien|Heian Kyō Alien]]'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当宣|1=bettō-sen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396753}} * {{ill|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|ja|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/押領使|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497733}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kebiishi]][[fa:کبیشی]][[it:Kebiishi]][[ja:検非違使]][[ko:게비이시]][[zh:檢非違使]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q497217]] Wikidata name: Kebiishi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''[[:en:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba]]'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|1=East Asian extra-statutory office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378315}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and were first established in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]] 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|1=militia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637390}} were abolished under [[:en:Emperor Kanmu|Emperor Kanmu]], the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]]. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|1=court nobles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10369708}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In [[:en:Kanpyō (era)|Kanpyō]] 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|1=Danjōdai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487784}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|1=Chōrei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11484277}}'', which diverged from traditional [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō law]]. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]]'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|1=Hokumen no Bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404509}}, a military organization of the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] era. Under the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the establishment of the [[:en:Rokuhara Tandai|Rokuhara Tandai]] further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], their authority was transferred to the [[:en:Samurai-dokoro|Samurai-dokoro]] of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa (Muromachi period)]] era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至徳|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Sa (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "[[:en:Rashomon (disambiguation)|Rashōmon (short story)]]" and "[[:en:In a Grove|In a Grove]]" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''[[:en:Heiankyo Alien|Heian Kyō Alien]]'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当宣|1=bettō-sen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396753}} * {{ill|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|ja|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/押領使|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497733}}

The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Sa (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "[[:en:Rashomon (disambiguation)|Rashōmon (short story)]]" and "[[:en:In a Grove|In a Grove]]" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''[[:en:Heiankyo Alien|Heian Kyō Alien]]'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kebiishi]][[fa:کبیشی]][[it:Kebiishi]][[ja:検非違使]][[ko:게비이시]][[zh:檢非違使]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q497217]] Wikidata name: Kebiishi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''[[:en:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba]]'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|1=East Asian extra-statutory office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378315}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and were first established in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]] 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|1=militia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637390}} were abolished under [[:en:Emperor Kanmu|Emperor Kanmu]], the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]]. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|1=court nobles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10369708}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In [[:en:Kanpyō (era)|Kanpyō]] 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|1=Danjōdai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487784}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|1=Chōrei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11484277}}'', which diverged from traditional [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō law]]. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]]'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|1=Hokumen no Bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404509}}, a military organization of the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] era. Under the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the establishment of the [[:en:Rokuhara Tandai|Rokuhara Tandai]] further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], their authority was transferred to the [[:en:Samurai-dokoro|Samurai-dokoro]] of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa (Muromachi period)]] era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至徳|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Sa (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "[[:en:Rashomon (disambiguation)|Rashōmon (short story)]]" and "[[:en:In a Grove|In a Grove]]" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''[[:en:Heiankyo Alien|Heian Kyō Alien]]'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当宣|1=bettō-sen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396753}} * {{ill|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|ja|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/押領使|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497733}}

; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. == Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "[[:en:Rashomon (disambiguation)|Rashōmon (short story)]]" and "[[:en:In a Grove|In a Grove]]" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''[[:en:Heiankyo Alien|Heian Kyō Alien]]'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
