[[Category:Extraordinary Government Officials of Ancient Japan]] [[Category:Criminal Procedure Law in Japan]] [[Category:Police history of Japan]] [[Category:Public security systems]] [[Category:History of Japan]] [[Category:National law enforcement agencies of Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:게비이시]] <!--dewiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:게비이시]] <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:게비이시]] {{wikidata link|Q497217}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kebiishi]][[fa:کبیشی]][[it:Kebiishi]][[ja:検非違使]][[ko:게비이시]][[zh:檢非違使]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kebiishi]][[fa:کبیشی]][[it:Kebiishi]][[ja:検非違使]][[ko:게비이시]][[zh:檢非違使]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q497217]] Wikidata name: Kebiishi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''[[:en:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba]]'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|1=East Asian extra-statutory office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378315}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and were first established in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]] 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|1=militia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637390}} were abolished under [[:en:Emperor Kanmu|Emperor Kanmu]], the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]]. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|1=court nobles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10369708}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In [[:en:Kanpyō (era)|Kanpyō]] 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|1=Danjōdai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487784}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|1=Chōrei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11484277}}'', which diverged from traditional [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō law]]. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]]'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|1=Hokumen no Bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404509}}, a military organization of the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] era. Under the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the establishment of the [[:en:Rokuhara Tandai|Rokuhara Tandai]] further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], their authority was transferred to the [[:en:Samurai-dokoro|Samurai-dokoro]] of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa (Muromachi period)]] era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至徳|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Sa (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "[[:en:Rashomon (disambiguation)|Rashōmon (short story)]]" and "[[:en:In a Grove|In a Grove]]" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''[[:en:Heiankyo Alien|Heian Kyō Alien]]'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kebiishi]][[fa:کبیشی]][[it:Kebiishi]][[ja:検非違使]][[ko:게비이시]][[zh:檢非違使]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kebiishi]][[fa:کبیشی]][[it:Kebiishi]][[ja:検非違使]][[ko:게비이시]][[zh:檢非違使]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kebiishi]][[fa:کبیشی]][[it:Kebiishi]][[ja:検非違使]][[ko:게비이시]][[zh:檢非違使]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q497217]] Wikidata name: Kebiishi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''[[:en:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba]]'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|1=East Asian extra-statutory office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378315}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and were first established in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]] 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|1=militia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637390}} were abolished under [[:en:Emperor Kanmu|Emperor Kanmu]], the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]]. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|1=court nobles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10369708}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In [[:en:Kanpyō (era)|Kanpyō]] 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|1=Danjōdai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487784}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|1=Chōrei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11484277}}'', which diverged from traditional [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō law]]. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]]'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|1=Hokumen no Bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404509}}, a military organization of the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] era. Under the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the establishment of the [[:en:Rokuhara Tandai|Rokuhara Tandai]] further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], their authority was transferred to the [[:en:Samurai-dokoro|Samurai-dokoro]] of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa (Muromachi period)]] era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至徳|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Sa (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "[[:en:Rashomon (disambiguation)|Rashōmon (short story)]]" and "[[:en:In a Grove|In a Grove]]" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''[[:en:Heiankyo Alien|Heian Kyō Alien]]'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当宣|1=bettō-sen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396753}} * {{ill|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|ja|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/押領使|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497733}}
{{translated page|ja|検非違使|version=103152179|comment=Imported full ja history}}

They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]]. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|1=court nobles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10369708}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In [[:en:Kanpyō (era)|Kanpyō]] 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|1=Danjōdai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487784}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|1=Chōrei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11484277}}'', which diverged from traditional [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō law]]. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''[[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]]'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|1=Hokumen no Bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404509}}, a military organization of the [[:en:Cloistered rule|Insei]] era. Under the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the establishment of the [[:en:Rokuhara Tandai|Rokuhara Tandai]] further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], their authority was transferred to the [[:en:Samurai-dokoro|Samurai-dokoro]] of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa (Muromachi period)]] era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至徳|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Sa (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "[[:en:Rashomon (disambiguation)|Rashōmon (short story)]]" and "[[:en:In a Grove|In a Grove]]" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''[[:en:Heiankyo Alien|Heian Kyō Alien]]'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.

'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō]] officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|1=East Asian extra-statutory office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378315}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and were first established in the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin]] 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|1=militia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637390}} were abolished under [[:en:Emperor Kanmu|Emperor Kanmu]], the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|1=court nobles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10369708}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In [[:en:Kanpyō (era)|Kanpyō]] 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by [[:en:Tenryaku|Tenryaku]] 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.

Under the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], the establishment of the [[:en:Rokuhara Tandai|Rokuhara Tandai]] further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], their authority was transferred to the [[:en:Samurai-dokoro|Samurai-dokoro]] of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa (Muromachi period)]] era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至徳|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>

; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. By the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], their authority was transferred to the [[:en:Samurai-dokoro|Samurai-dokoro]] of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the [[:en:Jōwa (Muromachi period)|Jōwa (Muromachi period)]] era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至徳|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; [[:en:Bettō|Bettō (Japan)]] : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Sa (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; [[:en:Captain (armed forces)|Daiyū]] : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; [[:en:Sub-lieutenant|Shōyū]] : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]] or [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]]. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "[[:en:Rashomon (disambiguation)|Rashōmon (short story)]]" and "[[:en:In a Grove|In a Grove]]" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''[[:en:Heiankyo Alien|Heian Kyō Alien]]'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当宣|1=bettō-sen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396753}} * {{ill|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|ja|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/押領使|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497733}}
{{translated page|ja|検非違使|version=103152179|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[de:Kebiishi]] [[it:Kebiishi]] [[ko:검비위사]] [[zh:檢非違使]] [[fa:کبیشی]] [[ja:検非違使]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:게비이시]] <!--dewiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:게비이시]] <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:게비이시]] {{wikidata link|Q497217}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Criminal Procedure Law in Japan]] [[Category:Extraordinary Government Officials of Ancient Japan]] [[Category:History of Japan]] [[Category:Judiciary of Japan]] [[Category:Military history of Japan]] [[Category:National law enforcement agencies of Japan]] [[Category:Police history of Japan]] [[Category:Public security systems]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kebiishi]][[fa:کبیشی]][[it:Kebiishi]][[ja:検非違使]][[ko:게비이시]][[zh:檢非違使]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kebiishi]][[fa:کبیشی]][[it:Kebiishi]][[ja:検非違使]][[ko:게비이시]][[zh:檢非違使]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kebiishi]][[fa:کبیشی]][[it:Kebiishi]][[ja:検非違使]][[ko:게비이시]][[zh:檢非違使]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q497217]] Wikidata name: Kebiishi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''{{ill|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|en|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|WD=Q952153|lt=Ban Dainagon Ekotoba}}'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding {{ill|Chion-in|en|Chion-in|WD=Q873645|lt=Chion-in}}]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō}} officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|1=East Asian extra-statutory office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378315}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and were first established in the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from {{ill|Kōnin (era)|en|Kōnin (era)|WD=Q1373070|lt=Kōnin}} 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|1=militia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637390}} were abolished under {{ill|Emperor Kanmu|en|Emperor Kanmu|WD=Q314846|lt=Emperor Kanmu}}, the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|en|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|WD=Q11452077|lt=Kan'i sōtō system}}. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|1=court nobles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10369708}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In {{ill|Kanpyō (era)|en|Kanpyō (era)|WD=Q1346292|lt=Kanpyō}} 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by {{ill|Tenryaku|en|Tenryaku|WD=Q1346281|lt=Tenryaku}} 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the {{ill|Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|WD=Q6867320|lt=Ministry of Justice}} (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|1=Danjōdai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487784}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|1=Chōrei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11484277}}'', which diverged from traditional {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō law}}. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''{{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō (Japan)}}'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|1=Hokumen no Bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404509}}, a military organization of the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=Insei}} era. Under the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, the establishment of the {{ill|Rokuhara Tandai|en|Rokuhara Tandai|WD=Q938069|lt=Rokuhara Tandai}} further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, their authority was transferred to the {{ill|Samurai-dokoro|en|Samurai-dokoro|WD=Q1143696|lt=Samurai-dokoro}} of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|en|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|WD=Q1146143|lt=Jōwa (Muromachi period)}} era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至徳|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; {{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō (Japan)}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Sa (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Captain (armed forces)|en|Captain (armed forces)|WD=Q19100|lt=Daiyū}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; {{ill|Sub-lieutenant|en|Sub-lieutenant|WD=Q10918728|lt=Shōyū}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan}} or {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashomon (disambiguation)|en|Rashomon (disambiguation)|WD=Q229142|lt=Rashōmon (short story)}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|en|In a Grove|WD=Q3015488|lt=In a Grove}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heiankyo Alien|en|Heiankyo Alien|WD=Q11482386|lt=Heian Kyō Alien}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当宣|1=bettō-sen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396753}} * {{ill|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|ja|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/押領使|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497733}}

During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|1=Hokumen no Bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404509}}, a military organization of the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=Insei}} era. Under the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, the establishment of the {{ill|Rokuhara Tandai|en|Rokuhara Tandai|WD=Q938069|lt=Rokuhara Tandai}} further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, their authority was transferred to the {{ill|Samurai-dokoro|en|Samurai-dokoro|WD=Q1143696|lt=Samurai-dokoro}} of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|en|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|WD=Q1146143|lt=Jōwa (Muromachi period)}} era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至徳|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; {{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō (Japan)}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Sa (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Captain (armed forces)|en|Captain (armed forces)|WD=Q19100|lt=Daiyū}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; {{ill|Sub-lieutenant|en|Sub-lieutenant|WD=Q10918728|lt=Shōyū}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan}} or {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashomon (disambiguation)|en|Rashomon (disambiguation)|WD=Q229142|lt=Rashōmon (short story)}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|en|In a Grove|WD=Q3015488|lt=In a Grove}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heiankyo Alien|en|Heiankyo Alien|WD=Q11482386|lt=Heian Kyō Alien}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}

[[de:Kebiishi]] [[it:Kebiishi]] [[ko:검비위사]] [[zh:檢非違使]] [[fa:کبیشی]] [[ja:検非違使]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:게비이시]] <!--dewiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:게비이시]] <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:게비이시]] {{wikidata link|Q497217}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Criminal Procedure Law in Japan]] [[Category:Extraordinary Government Officials of Ancient Japan]] [[Category:History of Japan]] [[Category:Judiciary of Japan]] [[Category:Military history of Japan]] [[Category:National law enforcement agencies of Japan]] [[Category:Police history of Japan]] [[Category:Public security systems]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kebiishi]][[fa:کبیشی]][[it:Kebiishi]][[ja:検非違使]][[ko:게비이시]][[zh:檢非違使]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kebiishi]][[fa:کبیشی]][[it:Kebiishi]][[ja:検非違使]][[ko:게비이시]][[zh:檢非違使]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kebiishi]][[fa:کبیشی]][[it:Kebiishi]][[ja:検非違使]][[ko:게비이시]][[zh:檢非違使]]
[[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''{{ill|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|en|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|WD=Q952153|lt=Ban Dainagon Ekotoba}}'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding {{ill|Chion-in|en|Chion-in|WD=Q873645|lt=Chion-in}}]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō}} officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|1=East Asian extra-statutory office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378315}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and were first established in the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from {{ill|Kōnin (era)|en|Kōnin (era)|WD=Q1373070|lt=Kōnin}} 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|1=militia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637390}} were abolished under {{ill|Emperor Kanmu|en|Emperor Kanmu|WD=Q314846|lt=Emperor Kanmu}}, the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|en|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|WD=Q11452077|lt=Kan'i sōtō system}}. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|1=court nobles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10369708}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In {{ill|Kanpyō (era)|en|Kanpyō (era)|WD=Q1346292|lt=Kanpyō}} 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by {{ill|Tenryaku|en|Tenryaku|WD=Q1346281|lt=Tenryaku}} 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the {{ill|Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|WD=Q6867320|lt=Ministry of Justice}} (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|1=Danjōdai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487784}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|1=Chōrei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11484277}}'', which diverged from traditional {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō law}}. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''{{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō (Japan)}}'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|1=Hokumen no Bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404509}}, a military organization of the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=Insei}} era. Under the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, the establishment of the {{ill|Rokuhara Tandai|en|Rokuhara Tandai|WD=Q938069|lt=Rokuhara Tandai}} further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, their authority was transferred to the {{ill|Samurai-dokoro|en|Samurai-dokoro|WD=Q1143696|lt=Samurai-dokoro}} of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|en|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|WD=Q1146143|lt=Jōwa (Muromachi period)}} era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至徳|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; {{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō (Japan)}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Sa (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Captain (armed forces)|en|Captain (armed forces)|WD=Q19100|lt=Daiyū}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; {{ill|Sub-lieutenant|en|Sub-lieutenant|WD=Q10918728|lt=Shōyū}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan}} or {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashomon (disambiguation)|en|Rashomon (disambiguation)|WD=Q229142|lt=Rashōmon (short story)}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|en|In a Grove|WD=Q3015488|lt=In a Grove}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heiankyo Alien|en|Heiankyo Alien|WD=Q11482386|lt=Heian Kyō Alien}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.

During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|1=Hokumen no Bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404509}}, a military organization of the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=Insei}} era. Under the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, the establishment of the {{ill|Rokuhara Tandai|en|Rokuhara Tandai|WD=Q938069|lt=Rokuhara Tandai}} further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, their authority was transferred to the {{ill|Samurai-dokoro|en|Samurai-dokoro|WD=Q1143696|lt=Samurai-dokoro}} of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|en|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|WD=Q1146143|lt=Jōwa (Muromachi period)}} era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至徳|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; {{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō (Japan)}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Sa (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Captain (armed forces)|en|Captain (armed forces)|WD=Q19100|lt=Daiyū}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; {{ill|Sub-lieutenant|en|Sub-lieutenant|WD=Q10918728|lt=Shōyū}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan}} or {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashomon (disambiguation)|en|Rashomon (disambiguation)|WD=Q229142|lt=Rashōmon (short story)}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|en|In a Grove|WD=Q3015488|lt=In a Grove}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heiankyo Alien|en|Heiankyo Alien|WD=Q11482386|lt=Heian Kyō Alien}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当宣|1=bettō-sen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396753}} * {{ill|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|ja|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/押領使|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497733}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kebiishi]][[fa:کبیشی]][[it:Kebiishi]][[ja:検非違使]][[ko:게비이시]][[zh:檢非違使]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kebiishi]][[fa:کبیشی]][[it:Kebiishi]][[ja:検非違使]][[ko:게비이시]][[zh:檢非違使]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kebiishi]][[fa:کبیشی]][[it:Kebiishi]][[ja:検非違使]][[ko:게비이시]][[zh:檢非違使]]
[[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''{{ill|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|en|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|WD=Q952153|lt=Ban Dainagon Ekotoba}}'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding {{ill|Chion-in|en|Chion-in|WD=Q873645|lt=Chion-in}}]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō}} officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|1=East Asian extra-statutory office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378315}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and were first established in the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from {{ill|Kōnin (era)|en|Kōnin (era)|WD=Q1373070|lt=Kōnin}} 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|1=militia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637390}} were abolished under {{ill|Emperor Kanmu|en|Emperor Kanmu|WD=Q314846|lt=Emperor Kanmu}}, the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|en|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|WD=Q11452077|lt=Kan'i sōtō system}}. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|1=court nobles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10369708}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In {{ill|Kanpyō (era)|en|Kanpyō (era)|WD=Q1346292|lt=Kanpyō}} 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by {{ill|Tenryaku|en|Tenryaku|WD=Q1346281|lt=Tenryaku}} 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the {{ill|Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|WD=Q6867320|lt=Ministry of Justice}} (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|1=Danjōdai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487784}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|1=Chōrei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11484277}}'', which diverged from traditional {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō law}}. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''{{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō (Japan)}}'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|1=Hokumen no Bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404509}}, a military organization of the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=Insei}} era. Under the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, the establishment of the {{ill|Rokuhara Tandai|en|Rokuhara Tandai|WD=Q938069|lt=Rokuhara Tandai}} further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, their authority was transferred to the {{ill|Samurai-dokoro|en|Samurai-dokoro|WD=Q1143696|lt=Samurai-dokoro}} of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|en|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|WD=Q1146143|lt=Jōwa (Muromachi period)}} era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至徳|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; {{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō (Japan)}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Sa (official)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Captain (armed forces)|en|Captain (armed forces)|WD=Q19100|lt=Daiyū}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; {{ill|Sub-lieutenant|en|Sub-lieutenant|WD=Q10918728|lt=Shōyū}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan}} or {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Daishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashomon (disambiguation)|en|Rashomon (disambiguation)|WD=Q229142|lt=Rashōmon (short story)}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|en|In a Grove|WD=Q3015488|lt=In a Grove}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heiankyo Alien|en|Heiankyo Alien|WD=Q11482386|lt=Heian Kyō Alien}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Kiyotaro Ogawa|title=Study of Kebiishi|publisher=Waseda Law Review|year=1939}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hidematsu Wada|title=Government Position Explanations|publisher=Kodansha Academic Bunko|year=1983}} == See Also == * {{ill|Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja|検非違使別当宣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi Bettō-sen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当宣|1=bettō-sen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396753}} * {{ill|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|ja|押領使|1=ōryōshi|2=ja|3=押領使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/押領使|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497733}}

During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''{{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō (Japan)|1=Bettō}}'' (chief officials).

Under the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, the establishment of the {{ill|Rokuhara Tandai|en|Rokuhara Tandai|WD=Q938069|lt=Rokuhara Tandai|1=Rokuhara Tandai}} further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, their authority was transferred to the {{ill|Samurai-dokoro|en|Samurai-dokoro|WD=Q1143696|lt=Samurai-dokoro|1=Samurai-dokoro}} of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|en|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|WD=Q1146143|lt=Jōwa (Muromachi period)|1=Jōwa (Muromachi period)}} era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至德|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>

; {{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō (Japan)|1=Bettō}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Q108778788|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Captain (armed forces)|en|Captain (armed forces)|WD=Q19100|lt=Daiyū|1=Captain (armed forces)}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; {{ill|Sub-lieutenant|en|Sub-lieutenant|WD=Q10918728|lt=Shōyū|1=Sub-lieutenant}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan|1=Minamoto clan}} or {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan|1=Taira clan}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Q133594905|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals.
