[[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''{{ill|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|en|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|WD=Q952153|lt=Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|1=Ban Dainagon Ekotoba}}'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding {{ill|Chion-in|en|Chion-in|WD=Q873645|lt=Chion-in|1=Chion-in}}]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō|1=Ritsuryō}} officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|1=East Asian extra-statutory office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378315}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and were first established in the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from {{ill|Kōnin (era)|en|Kōnin (era)|WD=Q1373070|lt=Kōnin|1=Kōnin (era)}} 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|1=Q11637390|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637390}} were abolished under {{ill|Emperor Kanmu|en|Emperor Kanmu|WD=Q314846|lt=Emperor Kanmu|1=Emperor Kanmu}}, the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|en|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|WD=Q11452077|lt=Kan'i sōtō system|1=List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles}}. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|1=court nobles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10369708}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In {{ill|Kanpyō (era)|en|Kanpyō (era)|WD=Q1346292|lt=Kanpyō|1=Kanpyō (era)}} 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by {{ill|Tenryaku|en|Tenryaku|WD=Q1346281|lt=Tenryaku|1=Tenryaku}} 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the {{ill|Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|WD=Q6867320|lt=Ministry of Justice|1=Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)}} (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|1=Danjōdai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487784}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|1=Q11484277|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11484277}}'', which diverged from traditional {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō law|1=Traditional Chinese law}}. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''{{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō (Japan)|1=Bettō}}'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|1=Hokumen no Bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404509}}, a military organization of the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=Insei|1=Cloistered rule}} era. Under the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, the establishment of the {{ill|Rokuhara Tandai|en|Rokuhara Tandai|WD=Q938069|lt=Rokuhara Tandai|1=Rokuhara Tandai}} further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, their authority was transferred to the {{ill|Samurai-dokoro|en|Samurai-dokoro|WD=Q1143696|lt=Samurai-dokoro|1=Samurai-dokoro}} of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|en|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|WD=Q1146143|lt=Jōwa (Muromachi period)|1=Jōwa (Muromachi period)}} era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至德|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>

Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.

The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the {{ill|Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|WD=Q6867320|lt=Ministry of Justice|1=Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)}} (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|1=Danjōdai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487784}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|1=Q11484277|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11484277}}'', which diverged from traditional {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō law|1=Traditional Chinese law}}. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''{{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō (Japan)|1=Bettō}}'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|1=Hokumen no Bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404509}}, a military organization of the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=Insei|1=Cloistered rule}} era. Under the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, the establishment of the {{ill|Rokuhara Tandai|en|Rokuhara Tandai|WD=Q938069|lt=Rokuhara Tandai|1=Rokuhara Tandai}} further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, their authority was transferred to the {{ill|Samurai-dokoro|en|Samurai-dokoro|WD=Q1143696|lt=Samurai-dokoro|1=Samurai-dokoro}} of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|en|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|WD=Q1146143|lt=Jōwa (Muromachi period)|1=Jōwa (Muromachi period)}} era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至德|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; {{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō (Japan)|1=Bettō}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Q108778788|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Captain (armed forces)|en|Captain (armed forces)|WD=Q19100|lt=Daiyū|1=Captain (armed forces)}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; {{ill|Sub-lieutenant|en|Sub-lieutenant|WD=Q10918728|lt=Shōyū|1=Sub-lieutenant}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan|1=Minamoto clan}} or {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan|1=Taira clan}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Q133594905|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashomon (disambiguation)|en|Rashomon (disambiguation)|WD=Q229142|lt=Rashōmon (short story)|1=Rashomon (disambiguation)}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|en|In a Grove|WD=Q3015488|lt=In a Grove|1=In a Grove}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heiankyo Alien|en|Heiankyo Alien|WD=Q11482386|lt=Heian Kyō Alien|1=Heiankyo Alien}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
== References == {{Reflist}}

[[File:Ban Dainagon Ekotoba - police.jpg|thumbnail|Depiction of ''{{ill|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|en|Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|WD=Q952153|lt=Ban Dainagon Ekotoba|1=Ban Dainagon Ekotoba}}'' showing a ''kebiishi'']] [[File:Honen shonin eden - Police.jpg|thumbnail|''Kebiishi'' guarding {{ill|Chion-in|en|Chion-in|WD=Q873645|lt=Chion-in|1=Chion-in}}]]
'''Kebiishi''' (検非違使, also pronounced ''kenbiishi'') were {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō|1=Ritsuryō}} officials in Japan classified as {{ill|Extra-statutory office|ja|令外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extra-statutory office|1=East Asian extra-statutory office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378315}}. The term refers to agents investigating and correcting violations (''hii'') on behalf of the emperor. Abbreviated as ''hii,'' they were civil servants of the ''kebiishi'' office. The Chinese-style titles (''唐名'') for ''sa'' and ''jō'' Ranks were ''Tingwei'' (廷尉). They managed security and governance in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} and were first established in the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. By the late Heian period, similar offices were also set up in the provinces.
== Overview ==
The ''kebiishi'' first appear in historical records from {{ill|Kōnin (era)|en|Kōnin (era)|WD=Q1373070|lt=Kōnin|1=Kōnin (era)}} 7 (816), indicating they were likely established around this time. Since the {{ill|militia|ja|軍団 (古代日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/militia|1=Q11637390|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637390}} were abolished under {{ill|Emperor Kanmu|en|Emperor Kanmu|WD=Q314846|lt=Emperor Kanmu|1=Emperor Kanmu}}, the court had effectively abandoned military forces. Consequently, crime in Kyoto increased, leading to the creation of the ''kebiishi'' by utilizing personnel from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} (Imperial Guard). ''Kebiishi'' were selected from the military officers (''bugan'') of the Emonfu to fulfill policing duties. They were appointed by {{ill|senji|ja|宣旨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senji|qq=}} (imperial decree) and did not have a specific {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|en|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|WD=Q11452077|lt=Kan'i sōtō system|1=List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles}}. However, ''kebiishi'' who reached the fifth Rank or higher gained access to the imperial court and became {{ill|tonōnin|ja|殿上人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tonōnin|1=court nobles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10369708}}, making the position a milestone for the advancement of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}}.
In 864, ''kebiishi'' offices were set up in the eastern and western marketplaces in Kyoto, where public punishments were conducted as deterrents.
In {{ill|Kanpyō (era)|en|Kanpyō (era)|WD=Q1346292|lt=Kanpyō|1=Kanpyō (era)}} 7 (895), two ''kebiishi'' offices were established within the left and right Emonfu. However, by {{ill|Tenryaku|en|Tenryaku|WD=Q1346281|lt=Tenryaku|1=Tenryaku}} 1 (947), they were consolidated into a single office for efficiency, housed in the left office only.
The ''kebiishi'' gradually expanded their authority by assuming responsibilities from other institutions like the {{ill|Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)|WD=Q6867320|lt=Ministry of Justice|1=Ministry of Justice (pre-modern Japan)}} (judiciary), {{ill|Danjō-dai|ja|弾正台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Danjō-dai|1=Danjōdai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487784}} (policing and oversight), and {{ill|Kyōshiki|ja|京職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōshiki|1=Right Capital Ward Officer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11374719}} (administration and governance of Kyoto). Over time, they wielded significant power.
By the late Heian period, the ''kebiishi'' introduced their customary legal practices, called ''{{ill|Chōrei|ja|庁例|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōrei|1=Q11484277|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11484277}}'', which diverged from traditional {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō law|1=Traditional Chinese law}}. During this time, much of the office's work shifted to the residences of the ''{{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō (Japan)|1=Bettō}}'' (chief officials).
During the late Heian period, they were overshadowed by {{ill|Hokumen no Bushi|ja|北面武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokumen no Bushi|1=Hokumen no Bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404509}}, a military organization of the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=Insei|1=Cloistered rule}} era. Under the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, the establishment of the {{ill|Rokuhara Tandai|en|Rokuhara Tandai|WD=Q938069|lt=Rokuhara Tandai|1=Rokuhara Tandai}} further weakened the ''kebiishi.'' By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, their authority was transferred to the {{ill|Samurai-dokoro|en|Samurai-dokoro|WD=Q1143696|lt=Samurai-dokoro|1=Samurai-dokoro}} of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1154406}}.
Despite this decline, the ''kebiishi'' retained certain administrative and judicial functions. Their role in tracking and apprehending criminals was taken over by the Samurai-dokoro during the {{ill|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|en|Jōwa (Muromachi period)|WD=Q1146143|lt=Jōwa (Muromachi period)|1=Jōwa (Muromachi period)}} era. Records suggest their judicial functions lasted until {{ill|Shitoku|ja|至徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=至德|WD=Q1207030}} 3 (1386). Historian {{ill|Shigeaki Mori|ja|森茂暁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigeaki Mori|1=Shigeaki Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540288}} identifies the ''kebiishi'' office's dissolution during the late Shitoku era.<ref>Shigeaki Mori, ''Research on Public and Military Relations in the Nanboku-chō Period'' (1984), pp. 321–353.</ref>
== Internal Positions ==
; {{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|WD=Q2900274|lt=Bettō (Japan)|1=Bettō}} : Equivalent to the chief officer (''kami'') in the {{ill|Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō Four-Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}}. The title was referred to as ''Dailijing'' (大理卿) in Chinese. The ''bettō'' supervised the ''kebiishi'' but was not a ''kebiishi'' themselves. Appointees were typically middle- to high-Ranking court nobles. ; {{ill|Sa (official)|ja|佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sa (official)|1=Q108778788|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q108778788}} : Equivalent to the second officer (''suke''). ''Sa'' positions were often held by officials from the {{ill|Emonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626338}} and served as de facto managers of the ''kebiishi'' office. ; {{ill|Captain (armed forces)|en|Captain (armed forces)|WD=Q19100|lt=Daiyū|1=Captain (armed forces)}} : Equivalent to a judge (''jō''), usually appointed from the {{ill|Sakaue clan|ja|坂上氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaue clan|1=Sakanoue clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10928477}} or {{ill|Nakahara clan|ja|中原氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahara clan|1=Nakahara clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11363164}}. ; {{ill|Sub-lieutenant|en|Sub-lieutenant|WD=Q10918728|lt=Shōyū|1=Sub-lieutenant}} : Equivalent to a junior officer (''jō'') and frequently held by samurai from prominent clans such as the {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan|1=Minamoto clan}} or {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan|1=Taira clan}}. ; {{ill|Daishi|ja|大志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishi|1=Q133594905|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q133594905}} and {{ill|Shōshi|ja|少志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Equivalent to clerks (''sakan''), usually occupied by young bureaucrats from prominent families. ; {{ill|Kantokuchō|ja|看督長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantokuchō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Managed prisons but later focused on apprehending criminals. Known for their distinct uniform featuring red ''kariginu'' and white staffs. ; {{ill|Anju|ja|案主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Administrative officers in the ''kebiishi'' office, initially limited to one but later increased in number.
== Cultural References == * {{ill|Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūnosuke Akutagawa|qq=}}'s stories "{{ill|Rashomon (disambiguation)|en|Rashomon (disambiguation)|WD=Q229142|lt=Rashōmon (short story)|1=Rashomon (disambiguation)}}" and "{{ill|In a Grove|en|In a Grove|WD=Q3015488|lt=In a Grove|1=In a Grove}}" portray ''kebiishi'' as police-like figures. * The video game ''{{ill|Heiankyo Alien|en|Heiankyo Alien|WD=Q11482386|lt=Heian Kyō Alien|1=Heiankyo Alien}}'' features ''kebiishi'' as player characters.
